name: Organic User Interfaces
short_description: A founding manifesto proposing computers with non-flat, deformable, and shape-changing displays where physical form-change is itself the interaction.
long_description: Organic User Interfaces (OUIs), articulated by David Holman and Roel Vertegaal in a 2008 Communications of the ACM special issue, are user interfaces built on non-flat displays whose surface is the locus of interaction. The manifesto sets out three design principles—input equals output, function equals form, and form follows flow—and three interface types: flexible/deformable, shaped, and actuated/kinetic. It matters as physical AI / interaction design because it reframes the screen from a flat output window into a malleable physical material, where bending, shaping, and actuating the device body become the primary mode of expression and control rather than indirect point-and-click.
ai_note: A vision/manifesto for deformable and shape-changing displays as an interaction technique; it describes no AI/ML running inside any system.

images.caption: Figure from the Holman & Vertegaal CACM article illustrating non-flat, deformable organic display concepts.
images.alt_text: Diagram of curved and flexible display surfaces being bent and shaped by hand, illustrating organic user interface concepts from the paper.

interactions.title: Bend the corner to turn the page
interactions.description: Instead of tapping a button, you flex a flexible display the way you'd dog-ear a magazine — a bend gesture at the edge becomes the navigation, so the act of deforming the surface is itself the input and output.

interactions.title: Rub one sheet against another to copy it
interactions.description: In the PaperWindows prototype the manifesto draws on, you hold two pieces of tracked paper and physically rub one against the other to transfer a window or copy a page across — the gesture mimics carbon-copy and stencilling.

interactions.title: Hold a window in each hand
interactions.description: Each sheet of paper is a separate display, so you work two-handed — holding one document up to read while flipping, stacking, or pointing one sheet at another to move content between them, like spreading printouts across a desk.

interactions.title: Touch a globe instead of a flat map
interactions.description: Under the 'shaped' branch of the manifesto, a display takes a permanent non-flat form chosen to fit its content — a spherical multitouch surface lets you spin and touch geographic data directly, with none of the distortion a flat map forces.

interactions.title: Let the device reshape itself to fit the task
interactions.description: The 'actuated / kinetic' principle imagines a body whose programmable shape changes under computer control — the object physically morphs to present the right affordance or to surface data, so form follows the flow of the interaction rather than staying fixed.

interactions.title: Tap and annotate paper that stays paper
interactions.description: Because the surface keeps paper's affordances, you tap an image on a sheet to open it and write directly on the page with a pen — digital content behaves like ink on a physical print rather than pixels behind glass.

interactions.title: A foldable map that creases where you fold it
interactions.description: A pocket-sized flexible display you unfold to reveal more canvas and crease to bookmark a spot — the same bend-as-input principle applied to navigation, where the physical fold marks your place and zooms the region under it.

interactions.title: A wristband that curls tighter to signal urgency
interactions.description: Built on the actuated/kinetic principle, a worn band that subtly changes its curl or stiffness against your skin to convey a notification's importance — form-change as a quiet, glanceable output channel with no screen at all.

sources.title: Organic user interfaces: designing computers in any way, shape, or form
sources.access_notes: CACM vol. 51, no. 6, pp. 48-55, June 2008. Authors: David Holman and Roel Vertegaal.

sources.title: Organic user interface
sources.access_notes: Summary of the OUI definition, three design principles, and three interface types (flexible/deformable, shaped, actuated/kinetic).
